topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt harsh, exposing every misplaced shadow in the room. I stood near the entrance, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white, watching the crowd mill about the canvases. My latest series, the ones I’d poured months of sleepless nights into, were hung haphazardly, bathed in a sickly yellow glow that utterly ruined the texture. A man with a slicked-back hairstyle was leaning in close to one piece, murmuring something that made my jaw tighten. I took a slow, deliberate breath, the air tasting stale and thin. It was an insult, this blatant disregard for my vision, this casual appropriation of my labor. I shifted my weight, a subtle, almost imperceptible tightening in my shoulders, a silent declaration that this entire spectacle was beneath my notice. I didn't need to speak; the sheer stillness radiating off me was enough to make people instinctively give me a wide berth.

[story 2]

"Look at this piece," a voice chirped, dripping with false enthusiasm. I felt a familiar, cold knot tighten beneath my ribs. They had framed my charcoal sketches—the ones I’d been refining for years—as if they were some kind of accidental discovery. I watched as a patron circled the display, nodding approvingly, their gaze lingering on the very lines I had painstakingly drawn. I walked slowly toward the center of the room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the polished floor. My gaze swept over the arrangement, cataloging the disrespect. My lips pressed into a thin, unyielding line. I felt a distinct hardening in my chest, a refusal to acknowledge the flimsy justification for this invasion of my space. I stopped directly in front of the central work, my posture rigid, radiating an absolute refusal to participate in their curated admiration.

[story 3]

I leaned against a marble pillar, observing the scene unfold with detached scrutiny. The sheer audacity of it—my carefully constructed world, reduced to mere decoration for some fleeting social event. A young woman pointed, her expression one of wide-eyed wonder, at a vibrant abstract I had labored over. I didn't move, just let my eyes drift over her, a slow, deliberate sweep that seemed to measure the distance between us. A faint, almost imperceptible curl touched the corner of my mouth, a gesture so slight it might have been a twitch, but it spoke volumes about the situation. I felt a profound sense of being overlooked, reduced to an object of someone else’s fleeting interest. My hands remained clasped behind my back, a barrier against any impulse to intervene, a silent judgment hanging heavy in the air around me.
